topic.name: Cricket History and Players
topic.description: This cluster focuses on notable cricketers and significant cricket events, highlighting their careers and contributions to the sport.

Document 1: Irfan Pathan is an Indian cricketer who made his debut for India in the 2003 / 04 Border-Gavaskar Trophy.
Pathan was instrumental in India's One-day international and Test series wins in Pakistan in 2004.
Document 2: Andy Ganteaume was a Trinidadian cricketer who played one Test match for the West Indies in 1948 as a batsman.
He scored 112 in his only Test innings, which left him with the highest Test batting average in history.
Document 3: Ron Saggers was an Australian cricketer who played for New South Wales.
He played briefly for the Australian team, playing six Tests between 1948 and 1950.
Document 4: Izzy Westbury is an international cricketer and former president of the Oxford Union.
She is captain of Middlesex women's first team and represented the Netherlands in one One Day International (ODI) in 2005.
Document 5: The Wisden Trophy is awarded to the winner of the Test cricket series played between England and the West Indies.
It was first awarded in 1963 to commemorate the hundredth edition of Wisden Cricketers' Almanack.
Document 6: Merv Harvey was a cricketer who played in one Test match for Australia in 1947.
His younger brother, Neil, was one of Australia's finest batsmen.
Document 7: George Dewhurst was a Trinidadian cricketer who played for West Indies before the team attained Test match status.
He was an influential and popular member of the Trinidad and West Indian sides.

topic.name: Bolliger & Mabillard Roller Coasters
topic.description: The documents discuss various roller coasters designed and manufactured by Bolliger & Mabillard, highlighting their specifications, history, and ride experiences.

Document 1: Goliath is a steel coaster roller coaster at the La Ronde located in Montreal, Quebec, Canada.
Designed by Bolliger & Mabillard, it reaches a maximum height of 174.8 feet.
Document 2: Nitro is a steel Floorless Coaster at the Adlabs Imagica amusement park located in Khopoli, Mumbai, India.
Manufactured by Bolliger & Mabillard, the roller coaster reaches a maximum height of 132 feet.
Document 3: A Wing Coaster is a type of steel roller coaster manufactured by Bolliger & Mabillard.
Development of the Wing Coaster began between 2007 and 2008.
Document 4: Batman: The Ride is a steel inverted roller coaster found in Six Flags theme parks.
Built by consulting engineers Bolliger & Mabillard.
Document 5: Patriot is a steel inverted roller coaster designed by Bolliger & Mabillard at the Worlds of Fun amusement park.
The ride lasts about 2 minutes and 18 seconds.
Document 6: Goliath is a steel Hyper Coaster located at the Six Flags Over Georgia amusement park.
Designed by Bolliger & Mabillard it reaches a maximum height of 200 feet.
Document 7: Intimidator is a steel roller coaster built by Bolliger & Mabillard at Carowinds.
The roller coaster is located in the Carowinds Plaza section of the park.

topic.name: Controversial Historical Events
topic.description: This topic encompasses significant and contentious events in history that have sparked public debate and controversy.

1: The Hole is the unofficial nickname of a facility operated by the Church of Scientology on Gold Base...
a place of confinement and humiliation where Scientology's management culture...
2: The Gunfight at the O.K. Corral was a 30-second gunfight between an outlaw group of Cowboys and lawmen.
It has come to represent a period in American Old West when the frontier was virtually an open range for outlaws...
3: Mary Jane Rathbun... was an American medical cannabis activist.
She helped pass San Francisco Proposition P (1991) and California Proposition 215 (1996) to achieve those goals.
4: Elizabeth Cresswell... was one of the most successful prostitutes and brothel keepers of the English seventeenth century.
Her bawdy houses were favoured by King Charles and his court as well as powerful figures in government...
5: Sogen Kato... was thought to have been Tokyo's oldest man until July 2010, when his mummified corpse was found.
The discovery of Kato's remains sparked a search for other missing centenarians lost due to poor record keeping...
6: The Cerro Maravilla murders... is the name given by the Puerto Rican public and media to describe the events that occurred on July 25, 1978.
The incident and subsequent events sparked a series of political controversies...
7: ContactPoint was a government database that held information on all children under 18 in England.
It was created in response to the abuse and death of eight-year-old Victoria Climbié in 2000...

topic.name: Administrative Law in Singapore
topic.description: The documents discuss various aspects of administrative law in Singapore, including the doctrine of legitimate expectation, judicial review, and constitutional mechanisms.

Document 1: The doctrine of legitimate expectation in Singapore protects both procedural and substantive rights.
The procedural dimension of the doctrine of legitimate expectation has been recognized by Singapore courts.
Document 2: The Constitution of the Republic of Singapore Tribunal was established in 1994 pursuant to Article 100 of the Constitution of the Republic of Singapore.
Questions referred to the Tribunal may concern the validity of enacted laws or of bills that have not yet been passed by Parliament.
Document 4: Threshold issues are legal requirements in Singapore administrative law that must be satisfied by applicants before their claims for judicial review.
Applicants must establish they have private standing if they seek to represent personal interests.

topic.name: Sports History and Athletes
topic.description: This topic encompasses the achievements and contributions of notable athletes and teams across various sports, highlighting their impact on the history of athletics.

Document 1: Willis Ward was a track and field athlete and American football player who was inducted into the University of Michigan Athletic Hall of Honor in 1981.
In football, Ward was only the second African-American to win a varsity letter for the Michigan Wolverines football team.
Document 2: The Houston Rockets are an American professional basketball team based in Houston, Texas.
The team was established in 1967, and played in San Diego, California for four years, before moving to Houston, Texas.
Document 3: Shareef Abdur-Rahim is an American retired professional basketball player.
He was also the director of player personnel for the Sacramento Kings of the National Basketball Association (NBA).
Document 4: The 1990 Toledo Rockets football team represented the University of Toledo during the 1990 NCAA Division I-A football season.
The Rockets were led by first-year head coach Nick Saban.
Document 5: John Beilein is an American college basketball coach and current men's basketball head coach at the University of Michigan.
Beilein has won 665 career games at four-year universities.
Document 6: Jon Scheyer is an American former basketball player, currently an assistant coach of the Duke Blue Devils men's basketball team.
He led his high school team to an Illinois state basketball championship as a high school All-American.
Document 7: Red Auerbach was an American basketball coach of the Washington Capitols, the Tri-Cities Blackhawks and the Boston Celtics.
Auerbach is remembered as a pioneer of modern basketball, redefining basketball as a game dominated by team play and defense.

topic.name: Fictional Character Analysis
topic.description: The documents explore various fictional characters from British television series and films, detailing their development, storylines, and characteristics.

1: Oliver Valentine is a fictional character from the BBC medical drama Holby City.
He was initially portrayed as 'the golden boy' compared to his sister.
2: Ivan Jones is a fictional character in the British soap opera Emmerdale.
The character is bisexual and he starts a sexual relationship with Paul Lambert.
3: A Nightmare on Elm Street is a 2010 American slasher film directed by Samuel Bayer.
The film revolves around a group of teenagers who are being stalked in their dreams by an enigmatic man named Freddy Krueger.
4: 'La Couchette' is the first episode of the second series of British dark comedy anthology Inside No. 9.
The episode is set in a sleeper carriage on a French train.
5: Lana Lang is a fictional character on the television series Smallville.
She has been a series regular since the pilot episode.
6: 'Attack of Mark's Clone' is the sixth episode of the second season of the American animated television series Ugly Americans.
In the episode, Callie creates a clone of Mark that will guarantee the Department of Integration's win in a bowling tournament.
7: Professor Frink is a recurring character in the animated television series The Simpsons.
He is Springfield's nerdy scientist and professor and is extremely intelligent, though somewhat mad and socially inept.

topic.name: Literary Works and Themes
topic.description: The documents explore various literary works, focusing on their themes, structures, and historical contexts.

Document 1: The Answer 's at the End is a song by English musician George Harrison
the song's plea for tolerance recalls Harrison 's 1970 hit song 'Isn 't It a Pity'
Document 2: Burnt Norton is the first poem of T. S. Eliot 's Four Quartets
the central discussion within the poem is on the nature of time and salvation
Document 3: Sonnet 102 is one of the 154 sonnets written by English playwright and poet William Shakespeare
the poet writes of why he has stopped showering his muse with flowery praise
Document 4: Sadhak Rāmprasād Sen was a Shakta poet and saint of eighteenth century Bengal
his bhakti poems, known as Ramprasadi, are still popular in Bengal
Document 5: In Praise of Limestone is a poem written by W. H. Auden
the poem has been the subject of diverse scholarly interpretations
Document 6: The Catalogue of Women is a fragmentary Greek epic poem attributed to Hesiod
the poem amounted to a compendium of heroic mythology
Document 7: The Tempest is a play by William Shakespeare
it is set on a remote island, where the sorcerer Prospero plots to restore his daughter Miranda

topic.name: Hydroelectric Power and Natural Resources
topic.description: The documents discuss various hydroelectric power facilities and their geographical and historical contexts, highlighting their roles in energy generation and environmental significance.

Document 2: The Pensacola Dam...is a multiple-arch buttress dam on the Grand River...for the purposes of hydroelectric power generation
It is Oklahoma's first hydroelectric power plant
Document 3: The Siah Bisheh Pumped Storage Power Plant...uses the pumped-storage hydroelectric method to generate electricity during periods of high energy demand
It was delayed from 1992 until 2001 and the first generator went online in May 2013
Document 7: Oroville Dam is an earthfill embankment dam...serves mainly for water supply, hydroelectricity generation and flood control
The dam impounds Lake Oroville, the second largest man-made lake in the state of California

topic.name: Cultural and Religious Sites
topic.description: The documents discuss significant cultural and religious sites that symbolize the intersection of different faiths and practices.

Document 1: Nagore Dargah is a major pilgrimage centre that attracts pilgrims from both Islam and Hinduism, symbolizing peaceful coexistence between the two religions.
The most prominent event celebrated at Nagore dargah is the Kanduri festival, a fourteen-day commemoration of the death anniversary of Shahul Hamid.
Document 2: The Jokhang is considered the most sacred and important temple in Tibet, currently maintained by the Gelug school, but accepting worshipers from all sects of Buddhism.
The temple's architectural style is a mixture of Indian vihara design, Tibetan and Nepalese design.
Document 6: The Chinese Rites controversy was a dispute among Roman Catholic missionaries over the religiosity of Confucianism and Chinese rituals during the 17th and 18th centuries.
The Jesuits argued that these Chinese rites were secular rituals that were compatible with Christianity, within certain limits, and should thus be tolerated.

topic.name: Contemporary Music Albums
topic.description: This cluster focuses on contemporary music albums across various genres and artists, highlighting their production, themes, and commercial performance.

Document 1: Discipline is the tenth studio album by American recording artist Janet Jackson
The album experimented with the electropop, house, and dance-pop genres
Document 2: We 're New Here is a remix album by American recording artist Gil Scott-Heron and English music producer Jamie xx
Incorporating dubstep and UK garage styles
Document 3: Mi Novia Se Me Está Poniendo Vieja is a Latin pop song written by Guatemalan singer-songwriter Ricardo Arjona

Document 4: Unhalfbricking is the third album by the British folk rock band Fairport Convention
It is seen as a transitional album in their history
Document 5: Kikuuiki is the fourth studio album by Japanese band Sakanaction
The album was nominated as one of the finalists for the CD Shop Awards
Document 6: Mediocre is the Grammy Award and Latin Grammy nominated debut album of Mexican singer and actress Ximena Sariñana

topic.name: Heritage Breeds and Practices
topic.description: This topic encompasses traditional animal breeds and their historical significance in agriculture and culture.

Document 2: The Auxois is a horse breed from eastern France.
The breed reached its peak in the 1930s, but by the 1960s began to decline with the advent of mechanization.
Document 3: The Florida Cracker Horse is a breed of horse from Florida in the United States.
Through the efforts of several private families and the Florida government, the breed was saved from extinction.
Document 4: The Kentucky Mountain Saddle Horse is a horse breed from the US state of Kentucky.
In 1989 the Kentucky Mountain Saddle Horse Association (KMSHA) was formed.
Document 5: The Parson Russell Terrier is a breed of small white terrier that was the original Fox Terrier of the 18th century.
It is a feisty, energetic terrier, suited to sports and able to get along with children and other animals.
Document 6: The Boreray is a breed of sheep originating on the St Kilda archipelago off the west coast of Scotland.
It is the rarest breed of sheep in the United Kingdom.
Document 7: Navajo rugs and blankets are textiles produced by Navajo people of the Four Corners area of the United States.
Navajo textiles are highly regarded and have been sought after as trade items for over 150 years.

topic.name: Major League Baseball Players
topic.description: The documents discuss various players in Major League Baseball, highlighting their careers, achievements, and personal backgrounds.

Document 1: Hack Wilson was an American Major League Baseball player who played 12 seasons for the New York Giants, Chicago Cubs, Brooklyn Dodgers and Philadelphia Phillies.
He was one of the most accomplished power hitters in the game during the late 1920s and early 1930s.
Document 2: Jedd Gyorko is an American professional baseball second baseman for the St. Louis Cardinals of Major League Baseball.
He previously played in MLB for the San Diego Padres.
Document 4: Philip Humber is an American former professional baseball pitcher.
He pitched for the New York Mets, Minnesota Twins, Kansas City Royals, Chicago White Sox, and Houston Astros in seven seasons in Major League Baseball.
Document 5: Guillermo Mota is a former Dominican professional baseball relief pitcher in Major League Baseball.
He pitched for the Montreal Expos, Los Angeles Dodgers, Florida Marlins, Cleveland Indians, New York Mets, Milwaukee Brewers and San Francisco Giants.
Document 6: Doug Fister is an American professional baseball pitcher for the Houston Astros of Major League Baseball.
He previously played in MLB for the Seattle Mariners, Detroit Tigers and Washington Nationals.
Document 7: Ryan Hanigan is an American professional baseball catcher for the Boston Red Sox of Major League Baseball.
He has caught two no hitters, both thrown by Homer Bailey.

topic.name: Popular Music Releases
topic.description: This cluster discusses various popular music releases, highlighting their artists, production details, and critical reception.

Document 1: Dance Again ... the Hits is the first greatest hits album of American entertainer Jennifer Lopez
The album received generally positive reviews from music critics
Document 2: 'Titanium' is a song by French DJ and music producer David Guetta, featuring vocals by Australian recording artist Sia
Critics were positive towards the song and noted it as one of the standout tracks from Nothing but the Beat
Document 3: 'Outta My Head' is a song recorded by British singer-songwriter Leona Lewis for her second studio album Echo

Document 4: 'Waka Waka (This Time for Africa)' is a song by Colombian singer-songwriter Shakira, featuring the South African band Freshlyground

topic.name: Railway Infrastructure and Operations
topic.description: This topic encompasses various aspects of railway systems, including locomotives, bridges, and railway lines across different regions.

Document 1: The LSWR M7 class is a class of 0-4-4 passenger tank locomotive built between 1897 and 1911.
Members of the class lasted in service until 1964, and two examples have survived into preservation.
Document 2: Barmouth Bridge is a single-track largely wooden railway viaduct that carries the Cambrian Coast Railway across the River Mawddach estuary.
The bridge is a Grade II * listed structure, and has one of the longest timber viaducts still in regular use in Britain.
Document 3: The Lillesand – Flaksvand Line was a 16.59-kilometer railway between Flaksvand and Lillesand in Aust-Agder, Norway.
The line opened on 4 June 1896, and remained in use until 15 June 1953.
Document 4: Skarnsund Bridge is a 1,010-metre long concrete cable-stayed bridge that crosses the Skarnsundet sound, in Inderøy, Norway.
The bridge has a span of 530 metres, making it the longest of its type in the world for two years.
Document 5: The Hell – Sunnan Line is a 105-kilometer-long railway line between Hell and Sunnan in Nord-Trøndelag, Norway.
The railway is the most heavily trafficked non-electrified line in Norway.
Document 6: The West Cornwall Covered Bridge is a wooden covered lattice truss bridge carrying the Sharon-Goshen Turnpike over the Housatonic River.
It was added to the National Register of Historic Places in 1975.
Document 7: Edinburgh Trams is a tramway in Edinburgh, Scotland, operated by Transport for Edinburgh.
Construction began in June 2008, and it opened on 31 May 2014.

topic.name: Professional Wrestling Events
topic.description: The documents discuss various professional wrestling pay-per-view events and notable tag teams within WWE and other wrestling promotions.

Document 1: Unforgiven (2006) was a professional wrestling pay-per-view event produced by World Wrestling Entertainment (WWE)
The event starred talent from the Raw brand.
Document 2: King of the Ring (1994) was the second annual King of the Ring professional wrestling pay-per-view (PPV) event produced by the World Wrestling Federation (WWF)
Owen Hart won the tournament by defeating Tatanka, the 1 – 2 – 3 Kid and Razor Ramon over the course of the evening.
Document 4: Taboo Tuesday (2005) was a professional wrestling pay-per-view event produced by World Wrestling Entertainment (WWE)
The main event was a Triple Threat match for the WWE Championship.
Document 3: The World 's Greatest Tag Team is a professional wrestling tag team
They won the WWE Tag Team Championship in February 2003.
Document 5: Gregory Helms is an American professional wrestler who is currently signed to Total Nonstop Action Wrestling (TNA)
He is best known for his time with World Wrestling Entertainment (WWE).

Best known for his work in World Wrestling Entertainment (WWE).
Document 7: Cody Rhodes and Goldust were a professional wrestling tag team that competed in WWE

topic.name: Colonial and Early American Leadership
topic.description: This topic encompasses the roles and contributions of various military and political leaders during the colonial period and early formation of the United States.

Document 1: Francis Nicholson was a British military officer and colonial administrator.
He served as lieutenant governor of the dominion in 1688 and was involved in colonial military actions.
Document 2: Preston Leslie was the 26th Governor of Kentucky and territorial governor of Montana.
He helped effect passage of laws admitting the testimony of blacks in court and providing for an educational system for recently freed slaves.
Document 3: Alexander Hamilton was a Founding Father of the United States and the founder of the nation's financial system.
He was the primary author of the economic policies of the George Washington administration.
Document 5: Angus McDonald was a prominent Scottish American military officer and frontiersman in Virginia.
He served in the French and Indian War and commanded a company of Scottish Highlanders.
Document 6: Zachary Taylor was the 12th President of the United States and a career officer in the United States Army.
He was a national hero as a result of his victories in the Mexican-American War.
Document 7: William S. Clark was a colonel during the American Civil War and a leader in agricultural education.
He became the third president of the Massachusetts Agricultural College.

topic.name: Royal Australian Air Force in WWII
topic.description: The documents discuss various units, commands, and notable figures associated with the Royal Australian Air Force during World War II.

Document 1: John Dundas was a British Second World War fighter pilot and flying ace.
Dundas joined the Royal Auxiliary Air Force in July 1938.
Document 2: Northern Area Command was one of several geographically based commands raised by the Royal Australian Air Force during World War II.
It was primarily responsible for air defence, aerial reconnaissance and protection of the sea lanes.
Document 3: No. 5 Operational Training Unit was formed to train bomber pilots and navigators for service in World War II.
The unit was established to convert recently graduated pilots from advanced trainers to combat aircraft.
Document 4: No. 79 Wing was a Royal Australian Air Force wing of World War II.
It took part in the New Guinea and North-Western Area Campaigns during 1944 – 45.
Document 6: Area commands were the major operational and administrative formations of the Royal Australian Air Force between 1940 and 1954.
The RAAF established four commands to begin with in 1940 – 41.

Document 1: First Flight is the fiftieth episode of Star Trek: Enterprise.

Document 2: Demons is the twentieth episode of the fourth season of Star Trek: Enterprise.

The two ships then pursue a lead to a Klingon research outpost.

topic.name: Naval Warships History
topic.description: This topic encompasses the historical development, design, and operational roles of various naval warships from different nations, particularly during the late 19th and early 20th centuries.

Document 1: SMS Undine was the last member of the ten-ship Gazelle class, built by the Imperial German Navy.
After the outbreak of World War in August 1914, Undine was deployed to the Baltic Sea for use as a coastal defense ship.
Document 2: The Chacal-class destroyer were a group of six large destroyers built for the French Navy during the 1920s.
The ships were initially split between the Mediterranean Squadron and the Second Squadron.
Document 3: Georgii Pobedonosets was a battleship built for the Imperial Russian Navy, the fourth and final ship of the Ekaterina II class.
She participated in the pursuit of the mutinous battleship Potemkin in June 1905.
Document 4: Design B-65 was a class of Super Type A cruisers planned by the Imperial Japanese Navy before and during World War II.
The cruisers were to play a key role in the Night Battle Force portion of the 'Decisive battle' strategy.
Document 5: SMS Bremen was the lead ship of the seven-vessel Bremen class, built by the Imperial German Navy.
At the onset of hostilities, she was attached to the fleet in the Baltic tasked with containing the Russians.
Document 6: Palestro was an ironclad warship, the second and final member of the Principe Amedeo class, built for the Italian Regia Marina.
She served primarily in Italy's colonial empire and did not see action.
Document 7: SMS Greif was an aviso built by the Imperial German Navy, the only ship of her class.
In 1912, she was reduced to a training ship for engine-room personnel.

topic.name: Historical Castles and Churches
topic.description: The documents discuss the history and architectural significance of various castles and churches across England and Sweden.

Document 1: Brough Castle is a ruined castle in the village of Brough, Cumbria, England.
The castle was built by William Rufus around 1092 within the old Roman fort of Verterae.
Document 2: Uppsala Cathedral is a cathedral located between Uppsala University and the River Fyris.
The cathedral dates to the late 13th century and at a height of 118.7 metres, it is the tallest church in the Nordic countries.
Document 4: Longtown Castle is a ruined Norman motte-and-bailey fortification in Longtown, Herefordshire.
Built around 1175 by Hugh de Lacy, possibly reusing former Roman earthworks.
Document 5: St Peulan's Church, Llanbeulan is a disused medieval church in Llanbeulan, in Anglesey, north Wales.
The nave, which is the oldest part of the building, dates from the 12th century.

topic.name: Historical Battles and Sieges
topic.description: The documents discuss various historical battles and sieges across different regions and time periods, highlighting military conflicts and their outcomes.

Document 1: The Battle of Burton Bridge was fought between Royalist and Parliamentarian forces at Burton upon Trent on 4 July 1643 during the First English Civil War.
The Royalists, led by Colonel Thomas Tyldesley, launched a cavalry charge across the bridge which succeeded in defeating the Parliamentarians.
Document 2: The Battle of Ohrid took place on 14 or 15 September 1464 between the Ottoman Empire's forces and Skanderbeg's Albania.
Skanderbeg won the resulting battle and his men earned 40,000 ducats after captured Ottoman officers were ransomed.
Document 3: The Roman – Parthian War of 58 – 63 was fought between the Roman Empire and the Parthian Empire over control of Armenia.
The war, which was the only major foreign campaign of his reign, began with rapid success for the Roman forces.
Document 4: The Battle of Lechaeum (391 BC) was an Athenian victory in the Corinthian War.
By launching repeated hit-and-run attacks against the Spartan formation, Iphicrates and his men were able to wear the Spartans down.
Document 5: The Battle of Bonchurch took place sometime in late July 1545 at Bonchurch on the Isle of Wight.
The battle was part of the wider Italian War of 1542 – 1546, and took place during the 1545 French invasion of the Isle of Wight.
Document 6: The Siege of Klis or Battle of Klis was a siege of Klis Fortress in the Kingdom of Croatia within Habsburg Monarchy.
The siege of the fortress, which lasted for more than two decades, was fought as a part of the Ottoman – Habsburg wars.
Document 7: The Battle of Huoyi was fought in China on 8 September 617 between the forces of the rebel Duke of Tang, Li Yuan, and the army of the ruling Sui Dynasty.
Li Yuan's cavalry, under the command of his two eldest sons, lured the Sui out of the protection of the city walls.

topic.name: Business and Industry History
topic.description: The documents explore significant historical developments and events in various industries, including brewing, banking, broadcasting, and seafood production.

Document 1: John Smith's Brewery in Tadcaster, North Yorkshire, England, produces beers including John Smith's, the biggest selling bitter in the United Kingdom since the mid-1990s.
Following a series of acquisitions in the post-World War II period, the company became one of the largest regional brewers in the country.
Document 2: The UK currently has the world's biggest digital radio network, with 103 transmitters, two national DAB ensembles and 48 local and regional DAB ensembles.
Digital radio in the United Kingdom is being promoted by radio stations and the broadcasting industry on the premise that it provides superior quality sound and a wider choice of radio stations.
Document 3: Jay Cooke & Company was a U.S. bank that operated from 1861 to 1873.
The bank helped underwrite the Union Civil War effort.
Document 4: The Enron scandal, revealed in October 2001, eventually led to the bankruptcy of the Enron Corporation.
Enron was cited as the biggest audit failure.
Document 6: Marine Harvest ASA is a Norwegian seafood company with operations in a number of countries around the world.
The group has a share of between 25 and 30% of the global salmon and trout market, making it the world's largest company in the sector.
Document 7: The Public Relations Society of America (PRSA) is a nonprofit trade association for public relations professionals headquartered in New York City.
PRSA created a definition of public relations in 1982, which was updated with a crowd-sourced version in 2011.

topic.name: Aviation and Aerospace Development
topic.description: The documents discuss various aircraft and automotive innovations, focusing on their design, development, and operational history in military and racing contexts.

1: The Howmet TX was designed to test the competitive use of a gas turbine engine in sports car racing.
The TX later set six Fédération Internationale de l 'Automobile land speed records for turbines after being retired from racing.
2: The Ikarus IK-2 was a 1930s high-wing, single-seat, monoplane fighter aircraft of Yugoslav design.
Just 12 production models were built, as the aircraft was obsolescent at the time it was brought into service.
3: The UK operated the McDonnell Douglas F-4 Phantom II as one of its principal combat aircraft from the 1960s to the early 1990s.
The Phantom was procured to serve in both the Fleet Air Arm and Royal Air Force in several roles including air defence, close air support, low-level strike and tactical reconnaissance.
4: The Northrop F-20 Tigershark was a privately financed light fighter, designed and built by Northrop.
Its development began in 1975 as a further evolution of Northrop's F-5E Tiger II, featuring a new engine that greatly improved overall performance.
5: The Lockheed Martin F-22 Raptor is a fifth-generation, single-seat, twin-engine, all-weather stealth tactical fighter aircraft developed for the United States Air Force.
The aircraft was designed primarily as an air superiority fighter, but also has ground attack, electronic warfare, and signals intelligence capabilities.
6: The Fairey Spearfish was a British carrier-based, single-engined, torpedo bomber/dive bomber that was ordered from Fairey Aviation for the Fleet Air Arm during World War II.
Designed during the war, the prototype did not fly until July 1945.
7: The Lavochkin La-7 was a piston-engined Soviet fighter developed during World War II by the Lavochkin Design Bureau.
Its first flight was in early 1944 and it entered service with the Soviet Air Forces later in the year.

topic.name: Medieval Church Leaders
topic.description: This topic encompasses the lives and contributions of notable medieval church leaders across different regions, highlighting their roles and influences in ecclesiastical and political matters.

Document 1: Domnall Gerrlámhach was a King of Dublin and member of the Uí Briain dynasty, indicating the intertwining of political and ecclesiastical authority in medieval Ireland.
Document 2: Cynesige was a medieval English Archbishop of York, known for building and adorning his cathedral and consecrating bishops.
Document 3: Cuthbert was an Anglo-Saxon Archbishop of Canterbury who held church councils and contributed to the elevation of the Diocese of York.
Document 5: Nothhelm was a medieval Archbishop of Canterbury who gathered materials for Bede's historical works and attended to ecclesiastical matters.
Document 6: Jocelin of Wells was a Bishop of Bath involved in royal administration and active in his diocese, showcasing the role of bishops in governance.
Document 7: Albin of Brechin was a prelate in Scotland who served as Bishop of Brechin, illustrating the local ecclesiastical leadership in medieval Scotland.

topic.name: Fungal Species Descriptions
topic.description: This cluster contains detailed descriptions of various species of fungi, including their taxonomy, morphology, and habitat.

Document 1: Entoloma austroprunicolor is a species of agaric fungus in the family Entolomataceae.
The species was first formally described in 2007 by Australian mycologist Genevieve Gates.
Document 2: Calbovista is a fungal genus containing the single species Calbovista subsculpta.
It fruits singly or in groups along roads and in open woods at high elevations.
Document 3: Coprinellus impatiens is a species of fungus in the Psathyrellaceae family.
The species was first described in 1821 as Agaricus impatiens by Swedish mycologist Elias Magnus Fries.
Document 4: Pholiota flammans, commonly known as the yellow pholiota, is a basidiomycete agaric mushroom of the genus Pholiota.
This species was first recognised in 1783 by the German, August Batsch.
Document 5: Phallus calongei is a species of stinkhorn mushroom.
It was described as new to science in 2009.
Document 6: Volvopluteus earlei is a species of mushroom in the family Pluteaceae.
The cap of Volvopluteus earlei is typically between 2.5 – 5 cm in diameter.
Document 7: Limnoperdon is a fungal genus in the monotypic family Limnoperdaceae.
The genus is also monotypic, as it contains a single species, the aquatic fungus Limnoperdon incarnatum.

topic.name: Tennis and Paralympic Athletes
topic.description: The documents discuss the achievements and careers of prominent tennis players and Paralympic athletes from Great Britain and Australia.

Document 1: Laura Robson is a British tennis player and an Olympic Mixed Doubles Silver Medalist.
Robson was named WTA Newcomer of the Year for 2012.
Document 2: Andy Murray is a Scottish professional tennis player currently ranked world No. 2 in singles.
Murray won a silver medal in the 2012 mixed doubles with Laura Robson.
Document 5: Marina Mohnen is a wheelchair basketball player who won a silver medal at the 2008 Summer Paralympics.
Mohnen won a gold medal at the 2012 Summer Paralympics in London.
Document 6: Priya Cooper is an Australian world champion disabled swimmer, winning nine Paralympic gold medals.
Cooper was twice the co-captain of the Australian Paralympic team.

topic.name: Classical Music and Performance
topic.description: The documents discuss significant works in classical music, focusing on operas, ballets, and church cantatas, highlighting their compositions, performances, and historical contexts.

Document 1: Robert le diable is regarded as one of the first grand operas at the Paris Opéra.
The opera was immediately successful from its first night on 21 November 1831.
Document 2: The Dying Swan is a solo choreographed by Mikhail Fokine in 1905.
The ballet has since influenced modern interpretations of Odette in Tchaikovsky's Swan Lake.
Document 3: Sie werden euch in den Bann tun, BWV 183, is a church cantata by Johann Sebastian Bach.
The work includes some unusual woodwind scoring.
Document 4: Du wahrer Gott und Davids Sohn, BWV 23, is a church cantata by Johann Sebastian Bach.
The cantata deals with healing the blind near Jericho.
Document 5: Du Friedefürst, Herr Jesu Christ, BWV 116, is a church cantata written by Johann Sebastian Bach.
It matches the Sunday’s prescribed gospel reading.
Document 6: Symphonic Odysseys was a symphonic tribute concert held in Cologne, Germany.
The concert exclusively paid homage to the work of Japanese composer Nobuo Uematsu.
Document 7: Sie werden aus Saba alle kommen, BWV 65, is a church cantata by Johann Sebastian Bach.
The text combines the prescribed readings for the feast day.

topic.name: Film Adaptations and Productions
topic.description: This cluster focuses on various films, their adaptations from other works, production details, and their impact on cinema.

Document 1: Let Me In is a 2010 American horror romance film...
It is a remake of the Swedish film Let the Right One In (2008).
Document 2: The Little Mermaid is a 1989 American animated musical fantasy film...
Based on the Danish fairy tale of the same name by Hans Christian Andersen.
Document 3: Stanley Kubrick was an American film director, screenwriter, producer...
His films are considered among the most important contributions to world cinema.
Document 4: Who Framed Roger Rabbit is a 1988 American live-action/animated fantasy comedy film...
The film is based on Gary K. Wolf's 1981 novel Who Censored Roger Rabbit?
Document 5: Veerapandiya Kattabomman is a 1959 Indian Tamil-language biographical film...
The film was an adaptation of the play Kattabomman.
Document 6: The Mambo Kings is a 1992 French-American drama film...
It is an adaptation of Oscar Hijuelos's 1989 Pulitzer Prize-winning novel.
Document 7: Friends with Benefits is a 2011 American romantic comedy film...
The plot revolves around Dylan Harper and Jamie Rellis, who meet in New York City.

topic.name: Faith and Ideology
topic.description: This cluster explores the intersections of faith, morality, and ideological movements, examining how beliefs shape individual and collective identities.

Document 1: Hitchens's journey from atheism to Christianity and conservatism
faith in God as a safeguard against moral chaos
Document 2: conspiracy theories about a New World Order and its ideological implications
secretive power elite with a globalist agenda
Document 3: Satanic and Left-Hand Path occult group espousing Neo-Nazi ideologies
links with other Neo-Nazi Satanist groups
Document 4: argument for the existence of God based on morality
God must exist to support moral order
Document 5: philosophical issues concerning consciousness and subjectivity
debates on whether consciousness can be mechanistically explained
Document 6: research in behavioral, neural, and psychiatric genetics
understanding behavior through genetic and neural frameworks
Document 7: emphasizes an empirical approach for learning about art
illustrates the benefits of scientific thinking

topic.name: Notable Historical Figures
topic.description: This cluster discusses influential individuals from various fields such as science, military, education, and healthcare who made significant contributions to society.

Document 1: Jeannette Piccard was an American high-altitude balloonist and Episcopal priest.
She was posthumously inducted into the International Space Hall of Fame in 1998.
Document 2: James Morris III was a Continental Army officer during the American Revolutionary War.
He founded the Morris Academy, a pioneer in coeducation.
Document 3: Barry was a St. Bernard dog credited with saving more than 40 lives during his lifetime.
His story and name have been used in literary works.
Document 4: Béla H. Bánáthy was the founder of the White Stag Leadership Development Program.
He became a widely read and respected author in the United States.
Document 5: Andrew Truxal was the third president of Hood College and first president of Anne Arundel Community College.
He is commemorated by a professorship and scholarship in Sociology at Hood College.
Document 6: Laura Bush was the First Lady of the United States from 2001 to 2009.
She implemented many initiatives focused on health, education, and literacy.
Document 7: Selma Mayer was the head nurse at the original Shaare Zedek Hospital in Jerusalem for nearly 50 years.
She became known as the 'Jewish Florence Nightingale' for her decades of selfless devotion to patient welfare.

topic.name: Engineering and Technology Concepts
topic.description: The documents discuss various engineering and technology concepts, including variable-frequency drives, amplifiers, and filter design.

1: A variable-frequency drive (VFD) is a type of adjustable-speed drive used in electro-mechanical drive systems to control AC motor speed and torque.
VFDs are used in applications ranging from small appliances to the largest of mine mill drives and compressors.
5: A double-tuned amplifier is a tuned amplifier with transformer coupling between the amplifier stages.
The scheme results in a wider bandwidth and steeper skirts than a single tuned circuit would achieve.
6: A composite image filter is an electronic filter consisting of multiple image filter sections of two or more different types.
Filters designed by this method are called image parameter filters, or just image filters.
7: Staggered tuning is a technique used in the design of multi-stage tuned amplifiers whereby each stage is tuned to a slightly different frequency.
It produces a wider bandwidth at the expense of reduced gain.

topic.name: Naval Battles of History
topic.description: This cluster discusses significant naval battles and maritime engagements from various historical periods, focusing on their strategic implications and outcomes.

Document 1: The Battle of Orbetello was a major naval engagement of the Franco-Spanish War of 1635.
It was fought on 14 June 1646 off the Spanish-ruled town of Orbetello.
Document 2: The Battle of Cape St. Vincent was a naval battle that took place off the southern coast of Portugal on 16 January 1780 during the Anglo-Spanish War.
A British fleet under Admiral Sir George Rodney defeated a Spanish squadron under Don Juan de Lángara.
Document 3: John Cooke was an experienced and highly regarded officer of the Royal Navy during the American War of Independence, the French Revolutionary Wars and the first years of the Napoleonic Wars.
He is best known for his death in hand-to-hand combat with French forces during the Battle of Trafalgar in 1805.
Document 4: HMS Emerald was a 36-gun Amazon-class frigate that Sir William Rule designed in 1794 for the Royal Navy.
She was part of Rear-Admiral John Thomas Duckworth's squadron during the Action of 7 April 1800, off Cadiz.
Document 7: HMS Alceste was a 38-gun frigate of the Royal Navy.
She continued to serve throughout the Napoleonic Wars and on 29 November 1811, she led a British squadron that captured a French military convoy bound for Trieste.

topic.name: Major Sporting Events
topic.description: The documents discuss notable sporting events including the IndyCar World Championship and the Boat Race between Oxford and Cambridge.

Document 1: 2011 IZOD IndyCar World Championship was the final race of the 2011 IZOD IndyCar series schedule.
the race was stopped following a major accident triggered by drivers.
Document 2: The 124th Boat Race between crews from the University of Oxford and the University of Cambridge took place on the River Thames.
Oxford won in a time of 18 minutes and 58 seconds.
Document 3: The 2009 AAA 400 was the twenty-eighth stock car race of the 2009 NASCAR Sprint Cup Series.
It was held on September 27, 2009 at Dover International Speedway.
Document 4: The 121st Boat Race between crews from the University of Oxford and the University of Cambridge took place on the River Thames.
Cambridge won their seventh race in eight years by three-and-three-quarter lengths.
Document 5: The 91st Boat Race took place on 1 April 1939.
Held annually, the Boat Race is a side-by-side rowing race between crews from the Universities of Oxford and Cambridge.
Document 6: The 80th Boat Race took place on 28 March 1928.
Cambridge won by ten lengths, the largest margin of victory since 1900.
Document 7: The 144th Boat Race took place on 28 March 1998.
Cambridge, with the heaviest crew in the history of the event, won by three lengths.

topic.name: Michigan State Highways
topic.description: The documents discuss various state trunkline highways in Michigan, detailing their routes, history, and geographical significance.

Document 1: M-52 is a north – south state trunkline highway in the US state of Michigan.
M-52 is an original state trunkline highway, dating to the 1919 formation of the system.
Document 2: M-311 is a 13.7-mile-long state trunkline highway in Calhoun County in the US state of Michigan.
M-311 was created on October 1, 1998 as a part of Governor John Engler's Rationalization process.
Document 3: M-8 is a 5.5 miles state trunkline highway in the U.S. state of Michigan lying within the cities of Detroit and Highland Park.
The roadway was transferred to the jurisdiction of the Michigan Department of Transportation (MDOT) in 1993 and numbered as M-8.
Document 5: County Road 510 (CR 510) is a primary county road in Marquette County, Michigan.
The road was originally part of the State Trunkline Highway System as M-35.

topic.name: Stingrays and Sharks
topic.description: This topic encompasses various species of stingrays and sharks, detailing their habitats, physical characteristics, feeding behaviors, and conservation statuses.

Document 1: The estuary stingray (Dasyatis fluviorum) is a species of stingray in the family Dasyatidae.
It mainly feeds on crustaceans and polychaete worms.
Document 2: The Izak catshark (Holohalaelurus regani) is a species of catshark, belonging to the family Scyliorhinidae.
It feeds mainly on a diverse array of bony fishes, crustaceans, and cephalopods.
Document 3: The nervous shark (Carcharhinus cautus) is a species of requiem shark, and part of the family Carcharhinidae.
Small bony fishes are the main prey of the nervous shark.
Document 5: The deepwater stingray or giant stingaree (Plesiobatis daviesi) is a species of stingray and the sole member of the family Plesiobatidae.
It preys on crustaceans, cephalopods, and bony fishes.

topic.name: Biodiversity and Evolutionary Adaptations
topic.description: This cluster discusses various organisms and their unique adaptations, highlighting their evolutionary significance and ecological roles.

1: Dragonflies are agile fliers and predators in both their larval and adult stages.
About 3000 species of Anisoptera are in the world today.
2: Kimberella is a monospecific genus of bilaterian known only from rocks of the Ediacaran period.
The classification of Kimberella is important for scientific understanding of the Cambrian explosion.
3: Protocarnivorous plants trap and kill insects but lack the ability to digest or absorb nutrients from their prey.
The demarcation between carnivorous and protocarnivorous is blurred by the lack of a strict definition.
4: Bryozoa are a phylum of aquatic invertebrate animals that are filter feeders.
5: Postelsia palmaeformis is a species of kelp and classified within brown algae.
It is one of the few algae that can survive and remain erect out of the water.
6: Roystonea regia is a species of palm native to southern Florida, Mexico, and parts of Central America.
It has a religious role both in Santería and Christianity.
7: Iridomyrmex, or the rainbow ant, is a genus of ant known for its ecological dominance.
Most of these ants are native to Australia and are known to disturb soil.

topic.name: World War II Military History
topic.description: The documents discuss various military units and individuals involved in World War II, highlighting their actions and contributions during the conflict.

1: Michael J. Daly was a United States Army infantry officer who received the Medal of Honor for his actions in World War II.
He participated in the D-Day landings at Omaha Beach and fought on to Germany.
2: The 2/9th Battalion was an infantry battalion of the Australian Army during World War II.
It took part in the Siege of Tobruk and the New Guinea campaign.
3: The 2nd Commando Regiment is an elite special forces unit of the Australian Army, involved in operations in Iraq and Afghanistan.
It was created to conduct offensive and recovery operations beyond the range and capability of other ADF elements.
4: The 66th Division was an infantry division of the British Army that served in the trenches of the Western Front during World War I and was reformed in 1939.
It was disbanded in 1940 without seeing active service in the Second World War.
5: The 26th Infantry Division was an infantry division of the United States Army that fought through France and into Germany during World War II.
It was involved in liberating the Gusen concentration camp before the end of the war.
6: The United Nations Memorial Cemetery is a burial ground for United Nations Command casualties of the Korean War.
It contains 2,300 graves and is the only United Nations cemetery in the world.
7: The Arnhem Oosterbeek War Cemetery is home to 1759 graves from the Second World War, primarily of Allied servicemen killed in the Battle of Arnhem.
It was established in 1945 to honor those who fought during the conflict.

topic.name: Video Game Development and History
topic.description: The documents discuss various video games, their development processes, gameplay mechanics, and historical context within the gaming industry.

Document 1: Elgato is a brand of consumer technology products.
Elgato is best known for a line of video-recording products called EyeTV.
Document 2: Final Fantasy Type-0 HD is an action role-playing game developed by Square Enix.
Type-0 HD is a high-definition remaster of the Japan-exclusive PlayStation Portable game Final Fantasy Type-0.
Document 3: Shin Megami Tensei: Nine is a Japanese role-playing video game developed by Atlus.
The gameplay has the player navigating a customized avatar in third-person through the virtual world of Tokyo.
Document 4: Atic Atac is an arcade-adventure video game developed and published by Ultimate Play The Game.
The game takes place within a castle in which the player must seek out the 'Golden Key of ACG.'
Document 5: Dr. Mario is a 1990 Mario arcade-style action puzzle video game designed by Gunpei Yokoi.
The game focuses on the player character Mario, who assumes the role of a doctor and is tasked with eradicating deadly viruses.
Document 6: Grand Theft Auto III is an open world action-adventure video game developed by DMA Design.
Set within the fictional Liberty City, the game follows Claude after he is left for dead.
Document 7: Super Mario Sunshine is a platform video game developed by Nintendo Entertainment Analysis & Development.
Mario is ordered to clean up Isle Delfino, using a device called FLUDD.

topic.name: Political Campaigns and Leadership
topic.description: This cluster discusses various political campaigns and leadership roles of notable politicians from different regions and parties.

Document 1: Tommy Thompson presidential campaign of 2008 began when the former Wisconsin Governor announced his candidacy for the Republican nomination.
Thompson centered his campaign in Iowa, focusing primarily on the issues of health care reform and the War in Iraq.
Document 2: Don Getty served as the 11th Premier of Alberta between 1985 and 1992.
As Premier, Getty was faced with an economic slowdown and falling energy prices, which hit Alberta's petroleum-dominated economy hard.
Document 3: Edward M. Burke is alderman of the 14th Ward of the City of Chicago.
Burke has been called Chicago's 'most powerful alderman' by the Chicago Sun-Times.
Document 4: John Key is the 38th Prime Minister of New Zealand, in office since 2008.
He has led the New Zealand National Party since 2006.
Document 5: The Ron Paul presidential campaign of 1988 began in early 1987 when former Congressman Ron Paul announced his candidacy for the Libertarian Party.
Paul ran on a platform that included non-interventionism in foreign conflicts and the abolition of the Federal Reserve.
Document 6: Andry Rajoelina was the President of the High Transitional Authority of Madagascar.
He became president during a political crisis, having held the office of Mayor of Antananarivo for one year prior.
Document 7: The 2012 United States Senate election in Massachusetts took place on November 6, 2012.
Incumbent Republican Senator Scott Brown ran for re-election to a first full term.

topic.name: Pulp Magazines and Fiction
topic.description: The documents discuss various pulp magazines and their contributions to fiction, including genres like science fiction, magic, and sports.

Document 1: Tales of Magic and Mystery was a pulp magazine which published five monthly issues from December 1927 to April 1928.
It was edited by Walter Gibson, and published a mixture of fiction and articles on magic.
Document 4: Wonder Story Annual was a science fiction pulp magazine which was launched in 1950 by Standard Magazines.
It was created as a vehicle to reprint stories from early issues of Wonder Stories, Startling Stories, and Wonder Stories Quarterly.
Document 5: Out of This World Adventures was a pulp magazine which published two issues, in July and December 1950.
It included several pages of comics as well as science fiction stories.

topic.name: Radiation and Environmental Safety
topic.description: The documents discuss incidents and phenomena related to radiation exposure, its effects, and environmental factors.

1: A radiation accident occurred in Samut Prakan Province, Thailand in January – February 2000.
the root cause of the accident, which resulted in ten people being hospitalized for radiation injury, three of whom died
5: most notable for his role in the Manhattan Project
lobbying for civilian control of nuclear weapons

topic.name: Nationalist Movements and Leaders
topic.description: The documents discuss various nationalist movements and their prominent leaders across different historical contexts.

Document 1: Raúl Alfonsín was an Argentine lawyer, politician and statesman who served as the President of Argentina.
He opposed both sides of the Dirty War and established the National Commission on the Disappearance of Persons to investigate the crimes.
Document 2: Zahir al-Umar was the virtually autonomous Arab ruler of northern Palestine in the mid-18th century.
He successfully withstood assaults by the Ottoman governors and expanded his domain.
Document 3: The Armenian Revolutionary Federation is an Armenian nationalist and socialist political party founded in 1890.
The ARF campaigns for the recognition of the Armenian Genocide and the right to reparations.
Document 4: Józef Zajączek was a Polish general and politician involved in the governance of the Congress Kingdom of Poland.
He became a radical supporter of the Constitution of 3 May 1791.
Document 5: The Polish Underground State was a collective term for underground resistance organizations in Poland during World War II.
It encompassed military resistance and civilian structures loyal to the Government of the Republic of Poland in exile.
Document 6: The Armed Boats Squadron Dubrovnik was crucial in the defense of Dubrovnik during the Croatian War of Independence.
It provided a resupply route for the Dubrovnik population and troops defending the besieged city.
Document 7: The Malagasy Uprising was a nationalist rebellion against French colonial rule in Madagascar from 1947 to 1948.
Coordinated surprise attacks were launched by Malagasy nationalists against military bases and French-owned plantations.

topic.name: The X-Files Episodes
topic.description: A collection of episodes from the science fiction television series The X-Files, focusing on paranormal investigations by FBI agents Mulder and Scully.

Document 1: 'Beyond the Sea' is the thirteenth episode of the first season of The X-Files.
The episode centers on FBI special agents Fox Mulder and Dana Scully who work on cases linked to the paranormal.
Document 2: 'Avatar' is the twenty-first episode of the third season of The X-Files.
The show centers on FBI special agents Fox Mulder and Dana Scully who work on cases linked to the paranormal.
Document 3: 'Monday' is the fourteenth episode of the sixth season of The X-Files.
The episode centers on FBI special agents Fox Mulder and Dana Scully who work on cases linked to the paranormal.
Document 4: 'Roland' is the twenty-third episode of the first season of The X-Files.
The episode centers on FBI special agents Fox Mulder and Dana Scully who work on cases linked to the paranormal.
Document 5: Marita Covarrubias is a fictional character on The X-Files.
She was initially introduced as an informant, leaking diplomatic information to FBI Special Agent Fox Mulder.
Document 6: 'Chinga' is the tenth episode of the fifth season of The X-Files.
The episode centers on FBI special agents Fox Mulder and Dana Scully who work on cases linked to the paranormal.
Document 7: 'Talitha Cumi' is the twenty-fourth episode and the season finale of the third season of The X-Files.
The episode centers on FBI special agents Fox Mulder and Dana Scully who work on cases linked to the paranormal.

topic.name: Tropical Cyclone Events
topic.description: The documents discuss various tropical cyclones, including hurricanes and tropical storms, their formation, meteorological history, impacts, and damages caused during specific seasons.

Document 1: Hurricane Ramon was a very intense Pacific hurricane that generated heavy rains in Southern California.
the remnants of Hurricane Ramon produced heavy rainfall that caused flooding in California, indirectly contributing to five traffic-related fatalities.
Document 2: Tropical Storm Bret was a short-lived tropical storm of the 2005 Atlantic hurricane season that made landfall in the Mexican state of Veracruz.
The storm dropped heavy rainfall along its path, peaking at 266 mm (10.67 inches), which caused flooding and one drowning death.
Document 3: The 1920 Atlantic hurricane season featured tropical storms and hurricanes only in the month of September.
Of the season's six tropical cyclones, five became tropical storms and four strengthened into hurricanes.
Document 4: Tropical Depression One was a weak tropical cyclone that struck Cuba and the Bahamas in May and June 1993.
It formed in the western Caribbean Sea on May 31 and produced heavy rainfall along its path.
Document 5: The September 1948 Florida hurricane was the most intense tropical cyclone to make landfall in the state since the 1935 Labor Day hurricane.
Severe destruction was reported on the island, with winds up to 90 mph (140 km/h) observed in Havana.
Document 6: Typhoon Oliwa was one of a record eleven super typhoons in the 1997 Pacific typhoon season.
Afterward, it slowly weakened, and after passing east of Okinawa, Oliwa turned northeast and struck Japan with winds of 85 mph (140 km/h).
Document 7: Hurricane Ignacio was the latest-forming first hurricane of a Pacific hurricane season since reliable satellite observation began in 1966.
The hurricane produced heavy rainfall in southern Baja California, killing two rescue workers from floodwaters.

topic.name: City Histories and Development
topic.description: The documents explore the historical development and significance of various cities across the United States.

Document 1: The history of Fairbanks, the second-largest city in Alaska, can be traced to the founding of a trading post by E.T. Barnette on the south bank of the Chena River on August 26, 1901.
The gold caused a stampede of miners to the area, and buildings sprang up around Barnette's trading post.
Document 2: Ashland is a city in Jackson County, in the State of Oregon.
Historically, its liberal politics have differed, often sharply, with much of the rest of southwest Oregon.
Document 3: Hyde Park Township is a former civil township in Cook County, Illinois, United States that existed as a separate municipality from 1861 until 1889.
During Chicago's initial explosive growth, it developed from an adjacent rural area to a developed residential, commercial and resort community.
Document 4: San Diego is a major city in California, in San Diego County, on the coast of the Pacific Ocean.
San Diego was the first site visited by Europeans on what is now the West Coast of the United States.
Document 5: Cave Junction, incorporated in 1948, is a city in Josephine County, Oregon, United States.
For thousands of years, the Takelma Indians inhabited the Illinois Valley.
Document 6: Teaneck was created on February 19, 1895 by an act of the New Jersey Legislature from portions of Englewood Township and Ridgefield Township.
Independence followed the result of a referendum held on January 14, 1895, in which voters favored incorporation by a 46 – 7 margin.
Document 7: Coral Springs, officially the City of Coral Springs, is a city in Broward County, Florida.
The city is part of the Miami metropolitan area, which was home to 5,564,635 people in 2010.
